data.title: Akamai Technologies: Growing Edge Opportunities In The Agentic AI Era
data.description:
Akamai Technologies, Inc. (AKAM), a pioneer in the Content Delivery Network (CDN), is a stock I have been following for years. During my first coverage of the stock in July 2020, the prevailing trend was the massive COVID-19-related surge in global internet traffic that ended up accelerating demand for AKAM's cloud security and CDN services.

Suffice it to say, the stock has not performed well since my first coverage, which may have to do with AKAM's continuous transformation into a cloud edge inference and security player, followed by the consistent decline in its legacy CDN business.

In the past five years, in fact, AKAM has been trading sideways and has been down over -12%. Nonetheless, the stock has gained some renewed momentum YTD as it trades around $93 per share as of late, possibly driven by the recent expansion into the AI inference cloud market.

I maintain my buy rating. My 1-year price target of $108.2 presents a 16% upside from the current price of $93.5 per share.

Financial Reviews

AKAM still appears to be in a transition into a newer, yet specialized edge inference cloud and security business.

Q3 revenue was $1.06 billion, a 5% YoY growth, driven primarily by two business segments. CIS (Cloud Infrastructure Services) saw a 39% YoY revenue growth to $81 million, while security revenue grew 10% YoY to $568 million. Both segments' growth offset the declining legacy CDN business, which fell 4% YoY to $306 million.

Meanwhile, profitability grew stronger in Q3, driven by operational efficiencies as well as the growth of higher-margin CIS business. In addition to Non-GAAP EPS growing by 17% YoY to $1.86, non-GAAP operating margins also saw a 31% improvement.

Liquidity and balance sheet remain solid. While the debt-to-equity ratio inched higher, at 1.1x, it is still relatively at a healthy level. AKAM ended Q3 with $1.8 billion of cash and short-term investments, with a significant contribution coming from its cash flow from operations (OCF), which was over $1.1 billion in the nine months ending Q3, similar to the same level last year.

I believe that the strong OCF, as well as the balance sheet, provide AKAM with enough dry powder to heavily invest in CAPEX to drive its new business. In that sense, AKAM is on track to achieve its target of investing in CAPEX for CIS infrastructure at 20% of its revenue target for the FY as per its earlier guidance. In the end, around $800 million was also spent in the first nine months of 2025 for share repurchases.

Catalyst

I see a few catalysts that could help AKAM maintain its uptrend share performance YTD as it continues its shift from a CDN to a cloud and security player. Given the declining legacy CDN business, these catalysts are primarily the growing trends around AI that may positively affect its CIS and Cloud Security business.

First off, I expect AKAM's edge AI inference technology to see growing demand due to many real-time use cases in enterprise AI that require better latency. Essentially, I would imagine many companies deploying agentic AI models into their operations across functions, demanding better connectivity.

As per the report by Gartner, 40% of enterprise applications will leverage AI agents in 2026, a 5% increase YoY. As such, while the cloud market appears to be saturated with players like Amazon AWS and Microsoft Azure, the AI inference cloud seems to be an underrated opportunity in 2026.

Therefore, in my opinion, while cloud security, AKAM's largest business today, continues to benefit from the increasing AI-driven cyber threats and grow in the low teens, AKAM's CIS business may continue to see sustained high growth around the 30% range, driving valuation re-rating.

Furthermore, the fact that AKAM only saw a -4% decline in the legacy CDN segment in Q3, which is a lower sequential decline compared to the previous two quarters, suggests that the CDN decline may be close to bottoming out. As a result, if the double-digit growth across CIS and Cloud Security is maintained throughout 2026, AKAM could soon be seeing less negative impact from the CDN decline, driving guidance beats.

Risk

Overall, risk seems minimal. While the 2026 outlook appears interesting, for instance, my view is that there is a likelihood that the market for edge cloud AI inference is developing more slowly than expected in 2026, since, despite the real AI trend, the enterprise agentic AI use cases are, for the most part, still in early stages. In that sense, companies would probably choose to deploy their inference AI inside their centralized hyperscaler servers, such as AWS or Azure, despite the latency benefits of edge cloud AI.

In the worst-case scenario, given the continued decline of CDN, the relatively high CAPEX investment AKAM has made to scale its CIS business, and the high-growth rate of the new CIS business that has been supporting the bullish case for re-rating, AKAM may struggle to earn its market premium into 2026.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for AKAM is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection based on the consensus estimate:

  1. Bull scenario (60% probability) assumptions—AKAM sees CIS growth accelerating to more than 40% YoY as more enterprises are adopting AKAM's edge AI approach to reduce latency, while the legacy CDN decline continues to slow down. I give AKAM a forward P/S of 4x, at the higher end of its historical trading range, suggesting that it should transition more into being valued as a cloud AI or security instead of a commodity CDN player.

  2. Bear scenario (40% probability) assumptions—AKAM sees CIS growth stagnant, with the heavy CAPEX investment that has been around 20% of revenue failing to yield the desired ROI. More enterprises choose centralized clouds instead of the edge to deploy AI models. I assign AKAM a forward P/S of 2.7x, not at the lowest end of its valuation range, but also lower than where it is today, suggesting that any hiccups in the new business means that it could more or less still be considered a commodity CDN competitor.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $108.2 per share, presenting a potential upside of 16% from the current level. At this point, I would give the stock a buy rating.

Conclusion

AKAM enters 2026 with an attractive market narrative, successfully transforming itself from a commodity CDN competitor into a fast-growing cloud security and AI edge inference player. With the two newer businesses thriving in the growing market due to the enterprise AI trend, I have a strong belief that AKAM could potentially see a re-rating in 2026. Despite the risk of hyperscalers taking the share of the AI edge market by offering a centralized alternative to enterprises, which I have built into my price target analysis, I think that risk remains minimal, and at this price point, AKAM is an attractive buy.

data.title: Carillon Eagle Growth & Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The equity market ended 2025 on a positive note. The S&P 500 Index returned 2.7% in the fourth quarter, finishing the year up 17.9%, just shy of a third consecutive 20%+ annual gain. This outcome seemed unlikely early in the year amid several potential disruptions, including the emergence of new competitive artificial intelligence (AI) models from China and heightened uncertainty following the rollout of a new U.S. tariff regime. Ultimately, strong AI‑related investment, the resumption of U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate cuts, and a more pragmatic tone from the Trump administration on trade negotiations allowed equities to push to new highs.

As in the prior two years, the communication services and technology sectors led performance, with a narrow set of mega‑cap technology, media, and telecommunications stocks contributing to approximately half of the S&P 500’s return. Still, market breadth improved meaningfully relative to 2023 and 2024. All 11 sectors posted positive returns, and the S&P 500® Equal Weight Index rose 11.4%. While this lagged the cap‑weighted index by 650 basis points (bps), the gap narrowed considerably from the 12% to 13% differentials of the prior two years. Notably, the top‑performing equity factor in 2025 was positive 3‑month earnings per share (EPS) revisions, which outpaced momentum, size, and growth. We view this as evidence that fundamentals still matter.

From a return‑driver standpoint, earnings growth was the clear engine of the market’s advance in 2025. Forward S&P 500 earnings are projected to rise 16% in 2026 over 2025. Dividends contributed roughly 1.5% to the total return, while valuation remained largely unchanged, with the index ending the year at approximately 22 times next 12‑month earnings.

Portfolio Review

Top securities

AstraZeneca (ANZ) reported third‑quarter financial results that surpassed analyst expectations and reaffirmed its outlook for operating margin improvements in 2026. The company also announced a constructive drug pricing agreement with the Trump administration, and presented impressive clinical trial results for a new drug to treat hypertension, which the company expects to have on the market by mid‑2026.

Lam Research (LRCX) shares benefitted from improving sentiment regarding the importance of its products within the semiconductor capital spending market. Following disappointments from semiconductor peers, Lam Research confirmed growth projections for next year, supported by bookings data. As a leading provider of equipment tied to memory requirements for AI, we believe Lam Research could have a long and healthy path to growth moving forward.

Goldman Sachs Group’s (GS) shares contributed to the fourth‑quarter performance due to positive financial results, coupled with increased optimism regarding capital markets activity heading into 2026. Goldman Sachs maintains one of the strongest global merger and acquisition (M&A) advisory and trading, with increased activity in M&A, initial public offerings, and debt issuance activity directly boosting its financial performance.

RTX’s (RTX) positive performance was a result of a strong third quarter earnings report that demonstrated improved revenue and earnings growth. Key end markets such as commercial aerospace and global defense are poised to continue to push excess growth for the company over the next several years. As such, we remain positive on RTX.

Analog Devices (ADI) pushed toward new all‑time highs after solid earnings gave investors' confidence that the analog cycle is now beyond its bottom. We believe the cycle is on track for an early recovery in 2026.

Bottom securities

Oracle (ORCL) gave up some of its gains after a big run to start the year over concerns about choppiness in AI spending and valuation pushbacks.

Eaton’s (ETN) shares pulled back in August following strong performance through July. Like many companies in the industrials complex, Eaton’s results did not exceed very high investor expectations. Moving into 2026, we anticipate accelerated growth for the company that is tied to strong trends in data center, aerospace, and defense markets.

Microsoft (MSFT) delivered weak quarterly performance, driven by industry‑wide concerns over a slowdown in software seat licenses, a key worry among investors.

T‑Mobile (TMUS) lagged amid uncertainty surrounding the strategic direction of one of its largest competitors. A new CEO at the competing company appears to have a more aggressive posture, which could pressure net subscription additions for T‑Mobile. However, in historical times of elevated switching behavior, T‑Mobile has tended to gain market share due to its well‑established value proposition.

Home Depot (HD) traded lower after reporting weaker‑than‑expected same‑store sales for the prior quarter. While weather‑related events boosted results in the prior quarter, the additional demand management anticipated failed to materialize. On a positive note, recent acquisitions continue to perform well and appear likely to be a tailwind as the business environment improves.

Outlook

With the S&P 500 up 86% for the past three years, it is reasonable to question the runway for further gains. However, as we enter 2026, we see more tangible tailwinds than headwinds and believe another year of positive equity returns is achievable.

Earnings remain the core pillar. Bloomberg projects S&P 500 EPS growth of 16% in 2026, up from 15% in 2025 – with 7 of 11 sectors expected to deliver double‑digit gains. AI investment remains robust and has the potential to broaden its impact across industries. Meanwhile, although tariffs remain a drag on year‑over‑year comparisons, companies have now had nine months since Liberation Day to redesign supply chains, adjust pricing, or mitigate cost pressures. The tariff anniversary mid‑year could further ease the growth impact. The macroeconomic backdrop remains constructive. Inflation continues to slow, the labor market is cooling without collapsing, and corporate profitability remains resilient. If the Fed maintains its data‑driven stance, monetary policy could be expected to gradually become more accommodative.

According to Bloomberg, futures contracts currently imply two 25‑bp cuts in 2026, which we view as a conservative starting point. Consumer strength remains a swing factor. A K‑shaped pattern persists, with lower‑income households pressured by the cumulative impact of inflation and softer wage growth. High‑income consumers – supported by wealth effects from rising asset prices – continue to spend consistently, and we do not see evidence this trend will change. Additionally, fiscal policies in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act should provide incremental stimulus beginning in early 2026.

Frothy equity valuations are often cited as a risk to the current bull market. At 22x forward earnings, the S&P 500 trades within one multiple of its 25‑year high. However, the market sustained this multiple throughout 2025, and accelerating earnings growth may further justify it. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index trades at a more modest 17.5x, suggesting the “average” stock is far less stretched and has the potential for broader market participation. The AI supercycle continues to provide powerful support, yet it carries risks. Investor willingness to underwrite aggressive AI spending has cooled somewhat, and the debate over whether we are in an AI bubble has increased. We expect AI‑related stock volatility to persist in 2026, but as long as forward revenue and earnings estimates continue to rise, the multi‑year AI investment cycle should remain intact. Policy remains a wildcard. With the unconventional administration, sudden shifts in tone or direction are possible. That said, mid‑term election years often bring a degree of policy restraint, which could reduce headline risk. Monetary policy uncertainty is another factor, particularly if inflation re‑accelerates. A new Fed chair is expected mid‑year, and this may also shape the policy path.

data.title: Lifeway Foods Is Still A Good Growth Stock Even If There's No Buyout
data.description:
My thesis for Lifeway Foods (LWAY) is that this kefir manufacturing company is undervalued in comparison to its growth potential. This isn’t the reason why other investors are interested in Lifeway Foods, though. Its CEO, Julie Smolyanski, has family members who are trying to gain control of the company and sell it to a large dairy-products company. I think Lifeway is a good growth stock regardless of whether a buyout happens, and the company’s latest results support this thesis.

In 3Q 2025, Lifeway reported that its GAAP revenue rose 24.0% to $57.1 million for the quarter, and its adjusted revenue growth came in at 29%. The company was guiding for 20% growth, so it beat estimates. The company’s gross margin also rose from 25.7% to 28.7%. And Lifeway also reiterated its guidance for full-year adjusted EBITDA of $45-$50 million in fiscal 2027. So, Lifeway’s margins are now rising even though its kefir factory won’t be fully upgraded until 2027.

But Lifeway’s stock price still fell in early January. I think this is because speculators sold Lifeway’s stock after their hopes of a quick buyout were dashed. Lifeway’s long-term growth prospects haven’t changed, and the company’s growth strategy is still on track. In fact, the company even reported preliminary 4Q 2025 numbers that suggest that its growth is accelerating. But in late December, the CEO’s relatives made another attempt to gain board seats and take over the company. Here’s how that takeover attempt went.

Julie Smolyansky Won Shareholder Vote in December

Lifeway Foods held its annual meeting on December 29, 2025. Before the meeting, the CEO’s brother Edward was nominated as a candidate to the board of directors, along with another opposition candidate, George Sent. Before the meeting, Lifeway sent shareholders a letter arguing that “Edward and Ludmila Smolyansky’s personal liquidity needs may be behind their demands for a quick sale of Lifeway at any price” and asked them to vote against the opposition nominees. The proxy advisory firm ISS also told shareholders to support Lifeway’s nominees before the vote.

Julie Smolyansky won the vote and kept control over the company. Then, in early January, Lifeway’s stock price fell some more. So, it looks like speculators were still buying Lifeway’s stock in December because it looked like the company could get bought out soon, and then they dumped the stock after the results of the annual meeting came out. My thesis is that these investors are being shortsighted, and Lifeway is still a good growth stock with its current CEO. And Lifeway’s institutional investors agree with this viewpoint as well.

Lifeway Foods Released Estimated Fourth-Quarter Results

In January, Lifeway Foods issued another press release guiding for full-year revenue of $211 to $212.5 million, or $211.75 million at the midpoint. Lifeway reported TTM revenue of $157.1 million in the third quarter, so the company is guiding for 4Q revenue of $54.65 million at the midpoint. Lifeway posted revenue of $46.9 million in 4Q 2024, so it now expects 16.5% revenue growth in the fourth quarter of 2025. And it posted $186.8 million in full-year 2024 revenue, so it’s guiding for 13.4% full-year revenue growth.

While Lifeway outperformed its guidance in the third quarter, this is a narrow guidance range, so it’s likely to be fairly accurate. So, it looks like Lifeway’s full-year 2025 revenue will be a bit less than I originally expected, and the company won’t report $220 million in revenue. But Lifeway’s revenue growth is still rising while other CPG companies are posting weaker fourth-quarter results. There are several reasons for this. Lifeway’s revenue is growing because of increased distribution, and it’s also increasing because the company is selling its Farmer Cheese in addition to kefir. This isn’t a one-product company. Next year, 16% revenue growth still seems very achievable, especially with the company launching another new product this year.

Lifeway Will Start Selling Kefir Butter in 2026

In January, Lifeway Foods announced that it will begin selling kefir butter in 2026. According to Food Dive, demand for premium butter has been rising, and full-fat butter is on-trend. The butter market is very competitive, but Lifeway believes that it’s found a unique angle by combining its expertise in kefir with its expertise in butter to create a new kind of probiotic butter. As the leading kefir company, Lifeway could use its kefir knowledge to introduce even more probiotic products in the future. It’s too soon to tell how popular the kefir butter will be, though.

Lifeway Still Plans to Invest in Business Over Next Few Years

Lifeway is still investing in its kefir factory and incurring short-term costs that are reducing its profitability to support its long-term growth. These investments will continue in 2026 and 2027. On the 3Q 2025 call, Julie Smolyansky said, “To date, in 2025, we have invested over $9 million in this project. And upon completion, we estimate the total investment of approximately $45 million in capital expenditures.” The company still expects to report $45-$50 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2027 but may still report lower margins than peers in 2026. It reported EBITDA of $6.1 million in 3Q 2025, so its EBITDA margin rose to 10.7%. But the median for consumer staples companies is currently 13.55%.

Lifeway is also incurring additional costs to expand its distribution to more retail store chains and develop new products, such as its kefir butter, which will also put pressure on its margins. And because of the weak consumer environment, this company may have limited pricing power in the short term. So, Lifeway’s margins might not show strong growth immediately, and there’s a reason why the CEO keeps talking about the company’s projected margins in 2027. But Lifeway’s stock isn’t trading at a premium valuation right now, either.

Family Disputes Remain the Largest Risk Factor

While the CEO’s relatives lost the vote in December, they still own stock in Lifeway and might not have given up on their plans to gain seats on Lifeway’s board and force the company to sell itself. The main risk here is that this dispute could distract the CEO and make it harder for her to lead the company effectively. This doesn’t appear to be the situation right now, though, because Lifeway has guided for strong 4Q 2025 growth, and its management has support from institutional investors. But if Lifeway’s growth slows down in 2026 because of economic weakness, activist investors might target the company because there’s already support for a buyout.

Lifeway Isn’t Receiving Much Growth Premium Right Now

Even though the CEO provided EBITDA guidance for 2027, I don’t think EBITDA is the right metric to use here because this company is investing in its plant and will have lower margins in the short term. So, I’ll use the EV/sales ratio again. After the market close on January 16, Lifeway had a forward EV/sales ratio of 1.46. That’s actually lower than the median for the consumer staples category at 1.49. Lifeway Foods’ valuation is also lower than the valuations of other beverage companies. On the same date, Monster (MNST) had a forward EV/sales ratio of 9.00. And large beverage makers trade at higher valuations as well. On the same date, Coca-Cola (KO) had a forward EV/sales of 6.99, and Pepsi (PEP) had a forward EV/sales of 2.62.

Lifeway does have a low gross margin in comparison to soft drink companies and energy drink companies, though, which justifies a lower EV/sales ratio. Even after the factory upgrades are complete, Lifeway’s margins may still be lower than other beverage companies’ margins. But completing the factory upgrades in 2027 would still improve this company’s efficiency and support multiple expansions.

Updated Valuation for Lifeway Foods

Lifeway Foods guided for $211.75 million in full-year 2025 revenue at the midpoint. So, for my updated 2026 valuation, I’ll project that Lifeway’s revenue will rise 16% to $245.6 million. Because its margins are likely to improve in 2027, I’ll project that its EV/sales ratio will rise to 1.6 in 2026. With a 2026 EV/sales ratio of 1.6, Lifeway would have an EV of $393.0 million. After subtracting $0.4 million in debt and adding back $23.0 million in cash, it would have a market cap of $415.6 million.

I’d also project that Lifeway’s share count will remain unchanged at 15.1 million shares in 2026. If I divide a market cap of $415.6 million by 15.1 million shares, that gives me a target price of $27.52 per share. The company’s market price after the market close on January 17 was $21.85, so my updated one-year price target is 26% higher than the current price. This is lower than my previous price target because I lowered my 2026 revenue growth estimate from 20% to 16%, but it still justifies a buy rating. Meanwhile, Wall Street says Lifeway is a strong buy with a price target of $35, up from $33 last time.

Conclusion

Lifeway’s CEO blocked the most recent attempt to gain control over the company and sell it to a larger competitor, so I still think it’s more appropriate to consider the company’s long-term growth prospects instead of speculating on a buyout. I still expect Lifeway to post very strong revenue growth for a CPG company in 2026, although it could take a few years before this translates into earnings growth as well. Lifeway is still on-trend with its probiotic food and is now planning to sell butter in addition to cheese and kefir. I still think this is one of the CPG stocks with the highest growth prospects and maintain my buy rating.
